name: Mortgage Payment Calculator
description: Calculate monthly mortgage payments including property tax, insurance & PMI. Free tool for any currency worldwide.
what_is: A mortgage calculator estimates your true total monthly home loan payment — not just principal and interest, but also property tax, homeowners insurance, PMI, and HOA fees (together known as PITI). It helps you understand the real cost of borrowing before you commit to one of the largest financial decisions of your life.
who_use: First-time homebuyers checking affordability, real estate investors analyzing financing costs, and anyone comparing loan terms, down payment sizes, or interest rates before applying for a mortgage.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the total home price. 2. Enter your down payment as a percentage of the home price. 3. Input your lender's annual interest rate. 4. Enter the loan term in years (e.g. 30). 5. Optionally add annual property tax, annual home insurance, and monthly HOA fees for a complete monthly payment estimate. 6. Click Calculate to see your full PITI breakdown.
how_to_read: The result shows your total monthly housing payment broken into Principal & Interest, Property Tax, Home Insurance, PMI (automatically added if your down payment is below 20%), and HOA fees. Keep your total housing cost below 28% of your gross monthly income for a healthy financial position. The amortization schedule below tracks your Principal & Interest portion only, matching how most lenders present it.
pro_tip: A down payment below 20% triggers Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI), an extra monthly cost with no equity benefit. If you're close to the 20% threshold, it's often worth delaying your purchase slightly to save the difference — this calculator shows exactly how much PMI adds to your monthly payment.

seo.meta_description: Calculate monthly mortgage payments with property tax, insurance & PMI included. Free tool works for any currency. Get accurate estimates now.

faqs.question: What is a safe monthly mortgage payment?
faqs.answer: Most financial advisors follow the 28% rule — your total monthly housing costs (PITI) should not exceed 28% of your gross monthly income. For example, on a $7,000 monthly income, keep housing costs below $1,960.

faqs.question: How is a monthly mortgage payment calculated?
faqs.answer: Principal and interest use the amortization formula M = P[r(1+r)^n]/[(1+r)^n-1], where P is loan amount, r is monthly interest rate, and n is number of payments. Property tax, insurance, PMI, and HOA are added on top.

faqs.question: What is PMI and when do I have to pay it?
faqs.answer: Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI) is typically required when your down payment is below 20% of the home price. It protects the lender, not you, and can usually be removed once you reach 20% equity.

faqs.question: Does this calculator include property tax and insurance?
faqs.answer: Yes. This calculator includes property tax, home insurance, PMI, and HOA fees alongside principal and interest, giving you a complete PITI monthly payment estimate.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a 15-year and 30-year mortgage?
faqs.answer: A 15-year mortgage has higher monthly payments but saves significantly on total interest. A 30-year mortgage has lower monthly payments but costs more over time.

faqs.question: Does this calculator work for different currencies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mortgage formulas work the same in any currency. Enter your values in your local currency to estimate monthly payment.

name: Personal Loan & EMI Calculator
description: Calculate monthly personal loan EMI, total repayment & interest cost. Free tool for any currency & lender worldwide.
what_is: A personal loan calculator helps you estimate exactly what you will owe each month before you commit to borrowing. Personal loans are unsecured credit products — meaning no collateral is required — commonly used for debt consolidation, medical expenses, home improvements, or major purchases. By entering your loan amount, APR, and repayment term, this tool instantly calculates your fixed monthly installment (EMI), total repayment amount, and lifetime interest cost so you can make a fully informed borrowing decision.
who_use: This calculator is built for anyone evaluating or comparing personal loan offers from traditional banks, credit unions, online lenders, or peer-to-peer (P2P) platforms. It is particularly useful for first-time borrowers learning how APR affects monthly payments, borrowers comparing multiple competing offers side by side, individuals planning a debt consolidation strategy, and anyone budgeting for a large upcoming expense like a wedding, relocation, or medical procedure.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your desired loan amount — the total principal you plan to borrow. Works with any currency including USD, GBP, EUR, AUD, INR, and more. 2. Input the annual interest rate (APR) quoted by your lender. If comparing offers, start with the lowest APR first. 3. Set your repayment term in months — for example, 12 months for one year or 60 months for five years. 4. Click Calculate to instantly see your fixed monthly payment, total amount repaid, and total interest charged over the loan life.
how_to_read: Your results will display three key figures. Your Monthly EMI is the fixed amount you owe your lender each month — this never changes for a standard fixed-rate personal loan. Your Total Repayment is the monthly EMI multiplied by your full loan term, showing the complete amount you will pay back. Your Total Interest Cost is the difference between total repayment and your original loan principal — this is the true cost of borrowing. A shorter loan term reduces total interest but increases your monthly payment. A longer term lowers the monthly payment but increases the total interest paid over time.
pro_tip: Always compare at least three lenders before signing. Even a 1% reduction in APR can save hundreds of dollars on a typical $10,000 loan. Focus on APR rather than just the monthly payment — a lower monthly payment with a longer term often costs significantly more in total interest. If your credit score is 720 or above, negotiate aggressively for a lower rate or use competing offers as leverage. Also check for origination fees, early repayment penalties, and prepayment options, as these can materially change your true borrowing cost.

faqs.question: What is considered a good debt-to-income ratio?
faqs.answer: Most lenders prefer DTI below 36%. For traditional mortgages, 43% is typically the maximum. Any DTI under 20% is excellent, positioning you for lowest interest rates and premium terms.

faqs.question: What monthly debts should be included in DTI calculation?
faqs.answer: Include all legally binding monthly obligations: rent/mortgage, auto loans, student loans, minimum credit card payments, child support/alimony, and personal loans. Exclude variable expenses like utilities or groceries.

faqs.question: How can I lower my DTI ratio quickly to secure loan approval?
faqs.answer: Eliminate smaller recurring balances entirely. Clearing a low-balance credit card or short-term loan completely removes that monthly payment from your debt column, immediately shrinking overall DTI.

faqs.question: Does my DTI ratio directly affect my credit score?
faqs.answer: No, DTI doesn't directly impact credit score—income data isn't part of credit bureau algorithms. However, high debt loads inflating DTI usually mean high credit utilization, which heavily impacts score.

name: Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) Calculator
description: Calculate DSCR for commercial loans, real estate & rental properties. Check loan approval eligibility.
what_is: The Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) is the primary financial benchmark commercial lenders and banks use to determine if a business or investment property generates sufficient cash flow to cover its annual debt obligations. This free global DSCR calculator computes your coverage score instantly so you can evaluate your commercial loan readiness before submitting a formal underwriting application.
who_use: Commercial real estate investors, property developers, rental property owners, and corporate business owners seeking commercial mortgages or structured business financing rely heavily on this tool. Understanding your DSCR score is non-negotiable when pitching to global lenders.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your asset's total Net Operating Income (NOI) (works with $, £, €, or any local currency). 2. Input your total Annual Debt Service (the sum of all principal and interest payments per year). 3. Click calculate to find your precise DSCR score.
how_to_read: A DSCR score of 1.25 or higher is the standard minimum benchmark mandated by most corporate lenders, providing a 25% financial cushion. A score of exactly 1.0 indicates your operating income perfectly equals your debt service—leaving zero cash cushion. Any score below 1.0 means the asset is operating at a net loss relative to its financing costs.
pro_tip: If your estimated DSCR is below 1.25, focus strategic energy on boosting your Net Operating Income (NOI) by lowering vacancies, optimizing operational expenses, or increasing rents before applying. Alternatively, inject a larger down payment to shrink your baseline Annual Debt Service. Model both financing paths with this calculator monthly!

faqs.question: What is minimum safe DSCR for commercial financing?
faqs.answer: Most banks mandate minimum DSCR of 1.25x, ensuring assets generate 25% more cash flow than debt obligations require. Alternative lenders may accept 1.15x, while high-risk properties might need 1.35x+.

faqs.question: How do I accurately calculate Net Operating Income (NOI)?
faqs.answer: Take gross annual rental/operating revenues and subtract necessary operational expenses (property taxes, insurance, maintenance, management fees, utilities). Don't subtract mortgage payments or depreciation.

faqs.question: Can I improve my DSCR profile before presenting to lenders?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Strengthen your ratio by maximizing NOI through optimized operations and leases, or mitigating debt service by negotiating longer amortization or introducing larger down payments.

faqs.question: How do residential rental DSCR loans differ from commercial?
faqs.answer: Same math formula. However, specialized residential lenders often prioritize property's rental cash flow over borrower's personal taxes/employment, sometimes approving loans with 1.0x to 1.2x ratios.

faqs.question: What is loan amortization and how does it function?
faqs.answer: Amortization is the structured mathematical method of clearing debt over fixed periods via regular, equal installments. While total monthly payment stays constant, each breakdown shifts: early payments pay mostly interest, later payments reduce core principal.

faqs.question: Why does such a large share of early payments go toward interest?
faqs.answer: At loan start, your outstanding principal balance is maximum, generating highest possible interest charges. As monthly installments reduce debt, the base for future interest shrinks, allowing larger percentage of payment to target principal.

faqs.question: How do extra principal payments alter my amortization schedule?
faqs.answer: Any extra payments apply 100% directly to outstanding principal. This skips ahead in the math, compressing the entire remaining amortization schedule and wiping out significant lifetime interest costs.

faqs.question: Are dividend distributions subject to taxation?

faqs.question: What is a good EPS for a stock?
faqs.answer: There's no universal 'good' EPS since pricing varies across industries. A company with lower nominal EPS could outperform if its growth velocity is faster. Always analyze EPS momentum quarter-over-quarter against industry peers.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Basic and Diluted EPS?
faqs.answer: Basic EPS uses only currently outstanding common shares. Diluted EPS includes all convertible securities (stock options, warrants, convertible bonds), providing a more conservative view of per-share earnings.

faqs.question: How does EPS relate to the P/E Ratio?
faqs.answer: P/E = Share Price ÷ EPS. If a stock trades at $50 with EPS of $2.50, P/E is 20. This shows investors pay $20 for every $1 of earnings, indicating whether the stock is over or undervalued.

faqs.answer: When driven by excessive debt. Since Equity = Assets minus Liabilities, massive debt shrinks the equity base, making ROE look excellent while carrying significant financial risk. Always cross-check debt ratios.

faqs.question: Why do software companies have higher ROE than manufacturing?
